item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: woooooooooorm hoooooooooles +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some things that occurred to me that maybe need clarification:

1- My list of reasons to downvote is extensive because I take the opportunity to put almost every detail I didn't liked. The reasons do not have the same weight, aren't in any particular order, and I don't need all of them "resolved" to change my mind.

2- You don't need my upvote and I don't have such delusion, in any way. If something doesn't make sense for you, feel free to go your own way. My main goal with critique is to help to improve works, even if the result is still outside my personal preferences.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is cool, and as others, like the author themselves, said, cool is more than reason enough to be on the mainlist.

But I have several problems with this that forced me into downvote territory. This is certainly one of the cases where I do want it to go negative, but not because I want it out, instead because I want it right.

+ show block
– hide block

1- Keter class is serious businesses, and any skip with them I'll want to be convinced of the necessity. The problem is that the way the sedation was played out didn't convinced me, what I'll explain later, and the sedation is the defining point for this being Keter or Euclid.

2- Ugly crossed out section that in the end seemed unnecessary. If you do want to let it this big, you could try to close it under a collapsible instead, but most of this text seems to be better somewhere else in the article. You could do something that mix both crossed-out and current Containment (the Foundation will still need the WWS to at least be alerted to the appearance of the thing, they cover more area during their usual activities), and then cross just the liaison and a little more.

3- I'm not too keen on the MTF named with a futhark rune's name instead of a number. At least you could put the rune properly, but it still put me on edge, again.

4- Scrantom isn't my favorite brand of Containment Equipment. Unless a skip tells a story that really needs fantastic science, I lean strongly on the hard sci-fi end on the Foundation part. To me they are anomaly containers, not the bureau of science authorization. If there is a special science that explains an anomaly, it ceases to be considered an anomaly and the Foundation would just let this new science be known. That's what an Explained anomaly is.

That put, the Equalizers end up doing nothing for the piece, since they actually got it under control with sedation during the time they did it at all, so it ends up deviating the focus of the article more than improving it.

5- The description focus on technobabble enough to actually become unnecessarily confusing. Among that confusion, some of the actual information that would contextualize the piece is lost. The portals are the meat of the anomaly, the thing that drives the story, and I don't know why so much people fell into it without realizing, I don't know how the sleeping thing could teleport drugs out of its own blood stream, I don't know how it moves so fast.

6- As much as I'm not against WWS being used here, how the Foundation dealt with them was more than a little off. An entire team of extra-dimensional WWS operatives went that far without the Foundation questioning their credentials? The status of the thing was discussed exclusively through a liaison that never appeared before? They are fighting this hard for their containment rights on such a dangerous thing?

7- The Boring Agreement excludes from WWS rights even Euclid-Class anomalies if too dangerous to handle. Keter-Class isn't even considered, even if it wasn't dangerous, just too hard to contain. Also, WWS is very limited in their actual containment facilities, so it is too much of a stretch to think they would want so badly something they obviously can't adequately maintain. If they fought just for rights on taking part on the containment process, as it happens to some Supervised SCPs, it would be more believable.

8- A containment breach caused by power failure is laughable if nothing else is given to contextualize this power failure. Put that way, I imagine this huge underground secret facility going through total collapse because of a little black-out during the no-break maintenance.

9- There is a build-up of extra-dimensional personnel and shit, but the entity seems to be unique. How many dimensions are sending teams to where this bug isn't yet, just for them to give chase when it arrives?

10- The discussion of containment rights goes on enough for it to actually harm the containment process. I cannot believe the fake liaison can achieve this just by representing WWS. The Foundation didn't took this kind of demand seriously from GOIs with far more power than WWS.

11- At what point this bug became a metalvore?

12- It took the Foundation 1 year and 3 days to… make a call to Mr. Wilson. A call that basically solved the entire conundrum when it was too late.

Sincerely, you can pull this off, but it will need more work. The fake liaison needs either more clotting, a better plan, or to act quickly so most of the events actually happened before someone could contact WWS through a phone call. The information on its anomalous properties must be clearer and more complete, or the lack of information should be better established. There is not enough doubt on the cause of the events that deviate too much from established properties. Maybe if the liaison were promptly recognized as an expert in the matter instead of just a representative of a modified animal shelter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Certainly it is something people would look for using the "insect" tag, so the tag is more than justified.

But it isn't genetically an insect at all, because millipedes aren't insects, not even close. Actually they are farther from insects than crustaceans are, they are not even that close to centipedes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The document for the Boring Nexus lists it as Briar, not Asphodel. Is that a mistake or is it part of the multiverse thing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tags aren't in-universe. It's not technically an insect, but it is one genetically, and it's the kind of thing people would be looking for when searching the insect tag. I made the call to keep it, based on the fact that someone who wants to find the giant millipede scip would probably want it to come up in the search results.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
l o n g b o i

How many legs does it have?

I like it, though it shouldn't be tagged with "insect".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As soon as I got to the "1.5 kilometers" bit, I spit out my Mountain Dew.
Interesting concept, like the thought of dimensional millipedes, another sterling appearance of WWS, +1.

EDIT- I did not mean to reply to the above post. This was accidental.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is the deal with Madison that she got transported to this universe from another and keeps releasing the millipede to try and return to her universe?

I love the whole article, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The millipede was fine, and probably enjoyed my suffering like the horrific scuttler it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was the millipede okay? 8[

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Story time. There's a thing we have here in merry old England called the National Trust, which is like a charity that preserves historical buildings, sites, and the like. There are quite a few stately homes within a not-too-long drive of where I live, so we go fairly often for a day out.

Now, the thing about National Trust properties is that they often run events and stuff. Tours and Easter egg hunts and things. On one occasion, it was a man with a load of different creatures in terrariums and cages, which he showed off in front of an audience of kids. I was an edgy 14 year-old at this point, and so stood at the back rolling my eyes and trying to look unimpressed.

Cue five minutes later, and he's letting people hold bugs. Specifically a giant millipede curled up into a ball, which he let people pass around. A small child then turns around and, without warning, places it into my hand. It starts to uncurl, and I can feel its legs on my skin, and I nearly drop it and scream. I couldn't though, so I had to hold it for about a minute before someone finally took it off me. As someone who mildly dislikes all kinds of bugs in general, and things with lots of legs specifically, it was awful. I'm not saying this was the subconscious inspiration for the scip, but it was totally the subconscious inspiration for the scip.

TL;DR: Millipedes are freaky and I have the anecdotes to prove it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because my fear of things with more than eight legs couldn't get any worse.

Upvoted due to scaring me without even trying.

item.title: Re: Needlessly defensive rebuttal
item.content:encoded: Finally got round to skimming over this again and the number of links seems much more reasonable to me.

item.title: Re: Needlessly defensive rebuttal
item.content:encoded:
I personally enjoy going from one article to the next, consuming as much information as possible. The cross-links, I believe, are very useful and good to have, even if not in the main article. In either case, I am not entirely aware of the WWS canon, so having the cross-linked information in the discussion thread is very helpful.

Leaving some cross-links in the main article would, of course, be useful as well. In the end, it's up to you as an author!

item.title: Re: Critique Turned Review
item.content:encoded:
Of course! I should have mentioned how long I take with critiques before asking you to make the draft thread.

And I respect that outlook. Not every SCP has to shatter the earth and force us to take a look at ourselves. We gotta have some exclusively-cool scips to even it out, so you're doing the Foundation a service.

item.title: Re: Critique Turned Review
item.content:encoded:
Oh man, you've gone above and beyond with this critique. As soon as I'm not dreadfully tired I'll make all the relevant changes. Thank you <3

And yeah, "just cool" is pretty much what I was going for. The wiki needs more "just cool" stuff in my opinion :)

EDIT: Changes have been made. I'll have to re-write the Harrison test at some point, because you're right — it is pretty contrived.

item.title: Critique Turned Review
item.content:encoded:
Started to critique the draft for this before I saw you'd published this. Nothing much was changed in-between, though, so I'll post this here:

Spelling, Grammatical, and Phrasing Corrections
Spelling, Grammatical, and Phrasing Corrections:

"As and when" here messed with my head, even if made sense after checking and rationalizing definitions.
Also, Mobile Task Force is singular:

As and when it re-manifests, Mobile Task Force Upsilon-Peorð ("Slings and Arrows") are is to track and sedate the entity

You abbreviate one unit and don't abbreviate the other. I suggest you keep it consistent:

at least 1.5 km kilometres in length and around 5 metres in diameter.

'Close' analysis doesn't sound clinical. 'Detailed,' maybe? 'Proximate?' Something more precise:

Close Detailed analysis of SCP-3MIL's biology has not been possible

This should be made its own sentence, and described passively, not with the imperative mood:

non-anomalous millipedes. It should be noted that, despite this, it is a genetic match with Harpaphe haydeniana and externally identical, excepting the additional body segments.

'Location' should be plural:

otherwise move quickly between locations.

This should be a footnote – as it is, it breaks the flow of an important sentence. This also calls for the removal of "that is to say," which I'd avoid in clinical writing:

That is to say A parallel universe that differs from baseline reality in one minor aspect, usually a different outcome to a specific event.

Establishing a parallelism here:

These wormholes are two-dimensional, largely imperceptible, and remain for some time temporarily stable after their following creation

Colon shouldn't be used here. You could replace it with a semicolon; but this sentence is so long, I recommend cutting it off with a period:

which organisation should take control of the anomaly. As per the Boring Agreement, Wilson's Wildlife Solutions had area priority

I think the record of all major events should be catalogued as an addendum.

Here, you can remove 'that' because it would be implied. I suggest you remove it because you've already used 'that:'

members of SCP-3MIL's containment team believed that US President William Henry Harrison died of an infected wound

"Containment lost" is a weird phrase. You should say "SCP-3MIL remains uncontained" or "breach lasts" or some other statement that SCP-3MIL isn't contained at this point:

Containment lost Breach lasts for approximately six weeks

I think this should be "places its date at." I can't cite any specific rule in grammar for this because I can't find one, so don't quote me on that. Here's how it would look:

Preliminary analysis places its date of creation at no more than five years previously.

'And' muddles causality here. Say "after which:"

Numerous crossbow bolts are embedded in the entity's back, and after which it appears severely weakened from extensive blood loss.

This is a roundabout way of saying "she died." The Foundation does not use euphemisms. Say "she died:"

a woman by that name had approached him for a position some years previously, but had been involved she died in a fatal car accident shortly afterwards.

The two things the Foundation wants to locate are PoI-7643 and the WWS team. Restructuring this sentence would make this point clear, and the writing more concise. Saying the location of the origin is redundant – 'origin' is defined as a place, so this is like saying "the location of the place." That the origin might be another universe is inherent. Here's how I'd rewrite it:

The location and origin of PoI-7643 ("Kimberly Madison Alternate") and the WWS research team responsible for initial categorisation and their universe(s) of origin are currently unknown.

Although the event on 29/07/2015 is both funny and interesting, I'm perplexed by how the nature and timing of William Henry Harrison's death was brought up in a "casual discussion" among Foundation personnel. It's not completely implausible – knowing my friends and colleagues, this might conceivably come up. But here, it seems to show up solely for convenience' sake.

I like this as a simple and original idea – a big -pede that burrows through other universes, hard to contain and understand. What makes this is how these complications seemingly pit the Foundation against the Wilson's Wildlife Solutions, whose dynamic piqued my interest when I first learned of them; but then there's the twist that they've been communicating with staff from an alternate universe. Your piece induced me to look up the Boring Agreement before continuing to read, and left me wanting to learn more about WWS.

Overall, I think this is cool. Not compelling, not evocative, not thought-provoking; just cool. I may be missing some aspect of this millipede – the title and other puzzling details hint at something more. But right now, this just reads as an interesting SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was only a matter of time before WWS mingled with universal shiz, and I am glad that is how this ended because Kimberly is really not the shining example of Wilson's good hearted nature. It is also interesting to see Wilson's acronym (WWS) appear officially in universe, used by the Foundation. +1, by the way.

item.title: Needlessly defensive rebuttal
item.content:encoded:
Ah, hmm. I guess I do have a tendency to overdo the crosslinks a little; rest assured that none of them are actually integral to understanding the article. My reasoning is as follows:

  • "as per the Boring Agreement" — this one is probably the most necessary of them, because it provides a link for users who don't know the WWS background. The nature of the agreement can be gotten from context (at least, I hope so), but I wanted to direct some attention to the scip that started it all.
  • "Class-E ("Momentary Lapse of Reason") wormholes" — this, I suppose, isn't really necessary. It's pretty clear from context, and would probably be better linked in the discussion page. Consider it changed.
  • "the Asphodel-class nexus" — same as before, really. It's not actually important, and I guess I could remove it without harming the flow of the article too much. On the other hand, the nexuses page is one I think deserves more attention, so the argument for this one is that I want to facilitate more interesting wiki-walks.
  • "Mobile Task Force Beta-4 ("Castaways")" — yeah, okay. I used weryllium's task force and wanted to give credit, but I guess an actual crosslink is a little gratuitous here. This one can probably go.
  • "the Foundation's Department of Extra-Universal Affairs" — I really want to keep this one. The Multi-U tales were a big part of what got me wanting to write this in the first place, and I'd much prefer giving them a mention in the article.

So yeah. I guess I did go a little overboard, huh. Hopefully I've cleared it up a little, and if you have any suggestions I'd love to hear them (it was probably a mistake to tie together so many different bits of canon in such a small article). Thanks for reading in any case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This sounds oddly familiar…

+ Yeah, I thought I read something similar earlier 
- Huh, weird

Event Description: Ten minutes before opening to the general public, three visit team staff and a security officer at ██████ Museum witnessed a Scutigera coleoptrata specimen, commonly known as a house centipede, emerge from a small drainage pipe in a storage closet. The specimen travelled approximately 1 m before entering a sink u-bend which had been opened for repairs. Specimen was estimated to be over 12 m in length, though of average width and height for its species. Specimen was visible for several minutes after the head portion had entered the u-bend, while the remainder of its body continued to exit the drainage pipe.

Anyways despite the fact that it's weirdly similar to a thing it's well written.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty good and the log contains some interesting bits but the number of links to external articles that seem to be required reading to fully get the context of what's going on here has tamped me down to a no-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice to see Multi-U show up in more articles. The many ways milliboi evades the Foundation were especially fun to read about.

+1

item.title: I like big bugs and I cannot lie
item.content:encoded:
So yeah. This is a thing I liked, so I posted it. Hopefully you guys like it too.

My everlasting thanks to…

  • DarkStuff, for creating Wilson's Wildlife Solutions and permitting me to screw them over. I'm proud to be the writer of the first article to involve them making things worse than they would have been otherwise.
  • The numerous people who critiqued this in chat, for getting this from my brain to a screen in a serviceable form. Thanks guys <3
  • TheBillith, for writing an excellent post about wormholes, and wormhole classifications.
  • Weryllium, for allowing me to use (and again, screw over) MTF Beta-4. Sorry bud.

In all seriousness, writing this was a blast, and I'm exceedingly happy to see it's staying afloat. The Megapede will persist!
